title: Systemic Warfare in the Networked Age
subtitle: Operational Systems, Information Competition, and Cumulative Pressure

alternate_identifiers.description: Short citation implied by the EPINOVA working paper format

description: This working paper introduces systemic warfare as a form of contemporary conflict in which actors impose cumulative pressure across interconnected operational systems and information domains rather than relying on decisive battlefield outcomes. It distinguishes Operational System Warfare as the material dimension of systemic conflict and information competition as the interpretive dimension, and develops the Operational Node Criticality Score (ONCS) and Systemic Pressure Index (SPI) as analytical constructs for modeling node-level importance and system-level stress.
abstract: Contemporary warfare is undergoing a structural transformation driven by the growing centrality of interconnected operational systems and networked information environments. Existing frameworks, from industrial warfare to network-centric warfare, emphasize either territorial control or information superiority, but provide limited tools for explaining how cumulative disruption across infrastructures generates strategic effects. This article introduces systemic warfare as a form of conflict in which actors seek to impose cumulative pressure across interconnected operational systems and information domains, rather than achieve decisive battlefield outcomes. Within this framework, Operational System Warfare (OSW) constitutes the material dimension of conflict, while information competition shapes its political and interpretive dynamics. To formalize this framework, the article develops two analytical constructs: the Operational Node Criticality Score (ONCS), capturing the structural importance of infrastructure nodes, and the Systemic Pressure Index (SPI), representing the nonlinear accumulation of system-level stress across interdependent infrastructures. These constructs are intended as analytical tools for modeling interaction mechanisms rather than as directly observed empirical measures. Drawing on observations from the initial phase of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, the analysis suggests that systemic pressure may accumulate nonlinearly as disruptions target increasingly critical nodes and propagate across interconnected systems. Sustained multi-domain actions can generate effects that appear to exceed the additive impact of individual strikes, reconfiguring the logic of warfare from discrete engagements toward system-level competition. The article contributes to the study of contemporary conflict in three ways: it reconceptualizes military effectiveness as system resilience under persistent disruption, introduces a formalized framework for analyzing cumulative pressure in networked conflict, and integrates operational and informational dynamics into a unified model of multi-domain warfare. More broadly, it argues that systemic warfare reflects a shift toward cumulative, system-level competition in which the interaction of material disruption and interpretive processes reshapes how strategic effects are generated.

series_information.series_description: The WP-F series explores AI governance and strategic risk through comparative and historical diagnostics, examining how inherited strategic and regulatory logics break down when transferred into AI-mediated contexts.

core_components: Operational System Warfare (OSW) as the material dimension of conflict
Information competition as the interpretive dimension of conflict
Operational Node Criticality Score (ONCS) for node-level strategic importance
Systemic Pressure Index (SPI) for aggregate system-level stress
Operational-information interaction as a recursive feedback loop
Multi-domain systemic effects across operational, information, cyber, and economic domains
methodology.method_type: Conceptual framework development with analytical modeling, illustrative simulation, and case illustration from the initial phase of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict.
methodology.analytical_focus: The paper models how localized disruptions to critical operational nodes can produce cumulative systemic pressure when propagated through interconnected infrastructures and amplified through information competition.
operational_information_interaction_sequ: Operational events such as strikes and deployments
Information circulation through media reports and social media
Narrative competition structured around legitimacy frames
Political pressure through public opinion and diplomacy
Strategic constraints such as escalation risk and alliance responses
Operational decisions including retaliation and adjustment

main_formulas.name: Operational Node Criticality Score
main_formulas.description: Captures the structural importance of an operational node based on functional importance, connectivity, dependency load, substitutability, and a scaling parameter.

variables.description: Functional importance of the node in sustaining operational capability
Connectivity of the node across subsystems
Dependency load; number and significance of subsystems reliant on the node
Substitutability expressed as inverse replacement availability
Scaling parameter for sensitivity across operational contexts

main_formulas.name: Systemic Pressure Index
main_formulas.formula: SPI_t = Σ_i (W_i × D_i × C_i × T_i × I_i)
main_formulas.description: Aggregates system-level stress across disrupted operational nodes, integrating node weight, disruption intensity, cascading multiplier, temporal persistence, and information amplification.

main_formulas.name: Cascading multiplier
main_formulas.description: Models how coupling among subsystems amplifies disruption propagation.

main_formulas.name: Information amplification factor
main_formulas.description: Represents how narrative salience across digital platforms amplifies the strategic effect of operational events.

main_formulas.name: Temporal persistence factor
main_formulas.description: Combines disruption duration with recovery delay to model persistence of systemic pressure.

main_formulas.name: ONCS-weighted SPI formulation
main_formulas.formula: SPI_t = Σ_i (ONCS_i × D_i × C_i × T_i × I_i)
main_formulas.description: Links node-level criticality directly to system-level pressure accumulation.

main_formulas.name: Reduced-form analytical specification
main_formulas.formula: ln(SPI_t) = α + Σ_{k=1}^{5} β_k × ln(X_{k,t}) + ε_t
main_formulas.description: Maps observable proxies for node criticality, disruption intensity, cascading effects, persistence, and informational amplification to systemic stress.

main_formulas.name: Interaction model specification
main_formulas.formula: ln(SPI_t) = α + Σ_{k=1}^{5} β_k × ln(X_{k,t}) + γ_1(ONCS_t × I_t) + γ_2(D_t × C_t) + μ_t
main_formulas.description: Introduces interaction terms for operational-information and disruption-cascade coupling.

main_formulas.name: Dynamic SPI recursion
main_formulas.formula: SPI_{t+1} = (1 − δ) × SPI_t + Σ_i (ONCS_i × D_{i,t} × C_{i,t} × T_{i,t} × I_{i,t}) − ρ_t
main_formulas.description: Models systemic pressure accumulation, dissipation, and recovery over time.

main_formulas.name: Threshold cascading multiplier
main_formulas.formula: C_{i,t} = c_1 if SPI_t < τ_1; c_2 if τ_1 ≤ SPI_t < τ_2; c_3 if SPI_t ≥ τ_2
main_formulas.description: Captures nonlinear transition from localized disruption to system-level stress.

main_formulas.name: Network pressure propagation
main_formulas.description: Extends pressure modeling to interdependent operational nodes.

main_formulas.name: Aggregate systemic pressure in network model
main_formulas.description: Defines total systemic pressure as ONCS-weighted pressure across operational nodes.

spi_patterns: High-criticality targeting with low frequency: efficient systemic pressure generation
Low-criticality targeting with high frequency: resource-intensive attrition
High-criticality targeting combined with strong information amplification: strategic-level effects extending beyond the operational domain

case_illustration.case: Initial phase of the 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict
case_illustration.scope_note: The case is used for analytical illustration rather than comprehensive empirical validation.

dimensions.name: Operational Systems
dimensions.description: Interconnected infrastructures that enable military forces to operate across regions, including bases, logistics, satellites, energy, digital communications, industrial supply chains, and alliances.

dimensions.name: Information Competition
dimensions.description: The interpretive domain in which narratives, legitimacy frames, platform amplification, and perception management shape how operational events are politically understood.

dimensions.name: Operational Node Criticality
dimensions.description: Node-level structural importance based on functional importance, connectivity, dependency load, and substitutability.

dimensions.name: Systemic Pressure
dimensions.description: Aggregate stress imposed on an operational system through node disruption, cascading interdependencies, temporal persistence, and information amplification.

dimensions.name: Interpretive Amplification
dimensions.description: Mechanism by which operational events are translated into political meaning through information networks and feed back into strategic constraints.

dimensions.name: Multi-Domain Systemic Effects
dimensions.description: Cumulative effects across operational, information, cyber, and economic domains.

key_findings: Contemporary warfare is shifting toward systemic warfare, in which actors impose cumulative pressure across interconnected operational systems and information environments rather than relying on decisive battlefield outcomes.
Operational System Warfare constitutes the material dimension of systemic warfare, targeting the infrastructures that sustain military operations across regions.
Information competition constitutes the interpretive dimension of systemic warfare, shaping how operational events are framed, amplified, and translated into political and strategic effects.
Operational Node Criticality Score (ONCS) provides a structured way to assess the systemic importance of infrastructure nodes within operational networks.
Systemic Pressure Index (SPI) models how disruption intensity, node importance, cascading effects, temporal persistence, and information amplification combine to produce aggregate systemic stress.
Systemic pressure may accumulate nonlinearly as disruptions target increasingly critical nodes and propagate across interconnected systems.

Operational and information dynamics form a recursive feedback loop: military operations generate observable events, information circulation creates narrative competition, and political pressure constrains subsequent operational decisions.
The 2026 U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict illustrates how operational disruption, information competition, cyber incidents, and economic pressure can interact across domains.
Military effectiveness in systemic warfare depends increasingly on system resilience, redundancy, recovery capacity, and the ability to manage perception within networked information environments.
Strategic outcomes are shaped less by isolated strikes than by cumulative pressure across interconnected systems over time.

figures.title: Operational System Architecture
figures.description: Layered diagram showing alliances, bases, logistics, sensors/ISR, satellites, energy, digital infrastructure, and industrial supply chains as interconnected components of operational systems.
figures.source: Author's conceptual framework based on the PDF
figures.note: Illustrates the distributed system structure through which localized disruptions can propagate across operational layers.

figures.title: Operational–Information Interaction
figures.description: Flow diagram linking military operations, operational events, information circulation, narrative competition, political pressure, strategic constraints, operational decisions, and feedback into subsequent operations.
figures.source: Author's conceptual framework based on the PDF
figures.note: Shows the recursive feedback loop between operational disruption and information competition.

figures.title: Simulated Trajectories of Systemic Pressure Under Escalation Scenarios
figures.description: Illustrative visualization showing strike count and modeled Systemic Pressure Index dynamics over time under escalation scenarios.
figures.source: Author's analytical simulation based on observed strike activity and information competition inputs described in the PDF
figures.note: Values are scaled for analytical illustration and do not represent calibrated real-world magnitudes.

figures.title: Platform Distribution of Conflict-Related Social Media Posts (February 28 – March 14, 2026, N = 5,000)
figures.description: Diagram showing X at 50% as the real-time narrative arena, Facebook at 24% as advocacy networks, TikTok at 16% as viral video flow, and Instagram at 10% as visual narrative.
figures.source: Author's structured analysis of 5,000 conflict-related social media posts described in the PDF
figures.note: Used to illustrate differentiated platform roles within the information competition dimension.

figures.note: Shows how systemic warfare extends across multiple interconnected domains.

disclaimer: This metadata record is generated from the uploaded PDF and early ORCID-derived metadata. The working paper presents diagnostic and exploratory analysis intended to examine inherited strategic and governance logics under AI-mediated conditions. It does not constitute policy recommendations, predictive assessments, or official positions of any institution.
